turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court today in case 17-773 Culbertson v. Barryhill.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a Writ of Certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit. Federal law regulates the fees that attorneys may charge to social security claimants for representation before the Social Security Administration and a reviewing court. The question in this case is whether 42 U.S.C. Section 406 limits the aggregate amount of fees for both stages of representation to 25% of the claimants' past-due benefits. Relying on binding circuit precedent the Eleventh Circuit held that the statute precludes the awards that in the aggregate are greater than 25% of past-due benefits. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we reverse. Section 406 (b) by its terms imposes a 25% cap on fees only for representation before a court and Section 406 (a) has separate caps on fees for representation before the agency. For this reason and others set out in our opinion we hold that statute does not impose a single 25% cap on fees in the aggregate. We, therefore, reverse the judgment of the Eleventh Circuit and remand for further proceedings. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
